sound_type: mass wing buzz over water -- thousands of mayflies emerging simultaneously from water surface, collective shimmering wing hum
ecosystem: Stream and lake. Clean freshwater habitats. Streams, rivers, ponds, and lakes with suitable substrate for nymphs.
season: Spring through autumn (species-dependent). Mass emergences typically in warmer months.

coordinates.description: Reference for stream/lake habitat in the PNW, Washington. Mayfly nymphs in clean streams and lakes throughout the Cascades and lowlands.

taxonomy_note.description: EPHEMEROPTERA -- NEW ORDER (7th insect order). NEW FAMILY (45th). First mayflies. Second aquatic insect order (after Plecoptera at 94). Most ancient winged insect lineage -- fossils 300+ million years old (Carboniferous).
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Ephemeroptera from Greek ephemeros ('short-lived, lasting a day') + pteron ('wing'). The name IS the biology -- adults live hours to days.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Family-level entry. Multiple Ephemeroptera families in PNW waters. The entry captures the mass emergence phenomenon.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Ephemeroptera -- NEW ORDER. Seventh insect order in the engine. Mayflies. Approximately 3,000 species worldwide. Among the most ancient winged insects -- Carboniferous fossils 300+ million years old. Unique subimago (dun) stage -- the only insects that molt after developing functional wings.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Insecta -- nine insect entries in degrees 87-95. The insect era extends. Seven orders represented.
taxonomy_note.family_position: 45th family at degree 95. NEW FAMILY. NEW ORDER. First mayflies. Second aquatic insect. Most ancient winged lineage.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Individual wing beat approximately 30-50 Hz. Mass emergence creates broad-spectrum shimmer from combined wing beats.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: No clear peak. Diffuse collective sound from thousands of wing beats.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. NOT a call. PHYSICAL SOUND of mass wing beats during synchronized emergence from water surface.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Mass emergence event: hours. Individual adult lifespan: hours to days.
song_metrics.note_structure: No note structure. Collective shimmering buzz. No rhythm, no pitch pattern -- the sound of thousands of delicate wings beating simultaneously over water.
song_metrics.note_types: Mass wing buzz (emergence sound). No vocalizations. No stridulation. Pure mechanical wing sound multiplied by thousands.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: No phrase structure. Continuous collective buzz during emergence event. Rises as more individuals emerge, fades as emergence completes.
song_metrics.timbre: Shimmering. Delicate but pervasive. A soft ambient hum. The sound of thousands of gossamer wings. Not harsh, not aggressive -- diffuse and ephemeral like the insects themselves.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Moderate. Mass numbers elevate individually inaudible wing beats to a noticeable collective sound. Not overwhelming but unmistakable during major hatches.
song_metrics.tempo: No tempo. Continuous. The mass buzz ebbs and flows with emergence intensity.
song_metrics.context: MASS EMERGENCE EVENT. Synchronized hatching. Thousands to millions emerging within hours. Predator satiation strategy. The sound occurs during the most vulnerable and spectacular moment of the mayfly life cycle.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Multiple Ephemeroptera species across PNW waters produce similar mass emergence events. Timing varies by species and water body.
song_metrics.key_property: MASS WING BUZZ OVER WATER -- the 96th acoustic element (species side). Thousands of mayflies emerging simultaneously. Collective shimmering buzz. E=3. Second aquatic insect (after stonefly 94). Most ancient winged insects. Ephemeral adults (hours to days). Fly fishing foundation. Stream/lake habitat.

physical_description.overall: Family-level entry. Adult mayflies: 4-30 mm body length depending on species. Elongated body. Two or three long cerci (tail filaments). Large triangular forewings held upright at rest (cannot fold flat). Hindwings small or absent. Large compound eyes.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 95: (1) WINGS HELD UPRIGHT at rest -- cannot fold flat, primitive trait. (2) TWO OR THREE LONG CERCI extending well beyond body length. (3) DELICATE BODY -- thin, translucent. (4) FOUND NEAR WATER during emergence -- mass numbers are diagnostic.
physical_description.skin_detail: Thin exoskeleton. Often translucent or pale. Wings with fine venation, often iridescent. Subimago (dun) has duller, more opaque wings than imago (spinner).
physical_description.dental_detail: Adult mouthparts are vestigial. Cannot eat. Nymphs have functional mandibles for feeding on algae and organic matter.
physical_description.field_id: Delicate insects with wings held upright at rest (like tiny sailboats). Long cerci (tail filaments). Found near streams and lakes. During mass emergences, thousands visible simultaneously -- covering surfaces, filling air above water. The mass emergence IS the field identification.

nodes.title: Mass Wing Buzz over Water -- 96th Acoustic Element (species side)
nodes.description: Collective shimmering wing buzz of thousands of mayflies emerging simultaneously. E=3. Mass emergence event. Individual wing beats inaudible; mass event produces distinctive ambient hum above water surface.

nodes.title: Ephemeroptera Enters -- 45th Family (NEW) / 7th Insect Order (NEW)
nodes.description: First mayflies. Second aquatic insect order (after Plecoptera at 94). Most ancient winged insect lineage -- 300+ million years. Seven insect orders in engine. Unique subimago stage.

nodes.title: Ephemeral Adult -- Hours to Days
nodes.description: Most ephemeral adult life in the engine. Cannot eat -- vestigial mouthparts. Entire adult purpose: emerge, mate, lay eggs, die. The name Ephemeroptera means 'lasting a day.'

nodes.title: Mass Emergence -- Predator Satiation Strategy
nodes.description: Thousands to millions hatching simultaneously. Overwhelms predators through sheer numbers. Synchronized by temperature and photoperiod. Visible on weather radar. One of nature's most spectacular events.

nodes.title: 300+ Million Year Lineage -- Carboniferous Fossils
nodes.description: Among the most ancient winged insects on Earth. Carboniferous period fossils. Predates dinosaurs, flowering plants, and most other insect orders. Primitive wing design -- cannot fold wings flat.

nodes.title: The ODESZA Contrast -- Euphoric Build vs Mass Wing Buzz
nodes.description: Degree 95 pairs mayfly with ODESZA in Part A. Euphoric build (E=5, electronic crescendo) vs mass wing buzz (E=3, natural crescendo). Both collective experiences -- festival crowd and mass emergence. Electronic architecture and ancient biology.

cross_references.relationship: STREAM SHARED! Both aquatic insects. Both water quality indicators. Back-to-back aquatic insect orders: Plecoptera (94) and Ephemeroptera (95). Both nymphs live in streams. Stonefly individual and silent (E=2); mayfly mass and buzzing (E=3). The aquatic insect pair.
STREAM CONNECTION. Kingfisher watches streams from above (57). Mayfly nymphs live in the streams below (95). The kingfisher may feed on emerging mayflies during mass hatches.
STREAM CONNECTION. Dipper forages in fast mountain streams where mayfly nymphs live. Predator-prey: dippers eat mayfly nymphs. Both require clean, cold water.
INSECT ERA CONTEXT. Both have aquatic larval/nymphal stages. Mosquito in still water; mayfly in streams and lakes. Different habitats, shared aquatic life cycle pattern.
INSECT ERA CONTEXT. Nine insect entries (87-95). The mayfly continues the aquatic turn that the stonefly began at 94. The insect era deepens in water.
